revision.text:
{{Infobox royalty | title = [[King of Wa]] | predecessor = [[Chin of Wa]] | successor = [[Ko of Wa]] | issue = [[Ko of Wa]], [[Bu of Wa]] | posthumous name = [[Emperor Ingyo]]? }} {{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}} {{Five kings of Wa}}
'''Sai of Wa''' ('''済''') was a king of [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}
Father of [[Ko of Wa|Ko]] and [[Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].
== Records ==
=== Book of Liang ===
; [[Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]] : In the [[Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。
=== History of the Southern Dynasties === In the [[History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles. {{Five kings of Wa Sources}}
== Historical investigation ==
=== On the continuity between Je and Chin === In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
=== Comparison of the tombs === During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Sakai|Sakai City]], [[Habikino|Habikino City]], [[Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Tumulus Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Tumulus Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[Emperor Ingyo]]).
Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} excavated from the {{Ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama burial mound]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama burial mound|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Bibliography ==
* 事典類 ** {{Cite book |last= |title=日本人名大辞典 |publisher=[[講談社]] |year= |isbn= |editor-last= |chapter=倭王済 |ref={{Harvid|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}}} ** {{Cite book |last=坂元義種 |title=[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] |publisher=[[吉川弘文館]] |year= |isbn= |editor-last= |chapter=倭の五王 |ref={{Harvid|倭の五王（国史）}}}} ** {{Cite book |last= |title=[[日本大百科全書]]（ニッポニカ） |publisher=[[小学館]] |year= |isbn= |editor-last= |chapter= }} *** 坂元義種 「倭の五王」、「済」。 ** {{Cite book |last=坂元義種 |title=日本古代史大辞典 |publisher=[[大和書房]] |year=2006 |isbn=978-4479840657 |editor-last= |chapter=倭の五王 |ref={{Harvid|倭の五王（古代史）|2006}}}} ** {{Cite book |last=関和彦 |title=朝日日本歴史人物事典 |publisher=[[朝日新聞出版]] |year= |isbn= |editor-last= |chapter= 倭王済|chapter-url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%80%AD%E7%8E%8B%E6%B8%88-1121323|ref={{Harvid|倭王済（朝日日本歴史人物事典）}} |author-link=関和彦}} - リンクは朝日新聞社「コトバンク」。 * その他文献 ** {{Cite book |last=森公章 |title=倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002） |publisher=[[山川出版社]] |year=2010 |isbn=978-4634548022 |editor-last= |chapter= |ref={{Harvid|森公章|2010}} |author-link=森公章}} ** {{Cite book |last=河内春人 |title=倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-（[[中公新書]]2470） |publisher=[[中央公論新社]] |year=2018 |isbn=978-4121024701 |editor-last= |chapter= |ref={{Harvid|河内春人|2018}}}} ** {{Cite news |last=[[石井正敏]] |date=June 2005 |title=5世紀の日韓関係 - 倭の五王と高句麗・百済 - |work=日韓歴史共同研究報告書（第1期） |publisher=[[日韓歴史共同研究]] |url=http://www.jkcf.or.jp/history_arch/first/1/1-03-ishii_j.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151018092747/http://www.jkcf.or.jp/history_arch/first/1/1-03-ishii_j.pdf |archive-date=2015-10-18 |ref={{Harvid|石井|2005}}}}

== External links == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm 漢籍電子文献資料庫] - 台湾中央研究院 {{Wiktionary|済}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Sai of Wa]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Sai of Wa]][[Category:Five kings of Wa]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Sai of Wa]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Sai of Wa]][[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Sai of Wa]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Sai of Wa]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Sai of Wa]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Sai of Wa]][[en:Sai of Wa]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Sai of Wa]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Sai of Wa]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Sai of Wa]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Sai of Wa]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Sai of Wa]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Sai of Wa]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Sai of Wa}][[en:Sai of Wa]][[en:Sai of Wa]]{{Yamatai}} [[Category:New]]

(or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama burial mound]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama burial mound|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

{{Infobox royalty | title = [[King of Wa]] | predecessor = [[Chin of Wa]] | successor = [[Ko of Wa]] | issue = [[Ko of Wa]], [[Bu of Wa]] | posthumous name = [[Emperor Ingyo]]? }} {{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}} {{Five kings of Wa}}
'''Sai of Wa''' ('''済''') was a king of [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}
Father of [[Ko of Wa|Ko]] and [[Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].
== Records ==
=== Book of Liang ===
; [[Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]] : In the [[Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。
=== History of the Southern Dynasties === In the [[History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles. {{Five kings of Wa Sources}}
== Historical investigation ==
=== On the continuity between Je and Chin === In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
=== Comparison of the tombs === During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Sakai|Sakai City]], [[Habikino|Habikino City]], [[Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Tumulus Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Tumulus Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[Emperor Ingyo]]).
Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} excavated from the {{Ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama burial mound]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama burial mound|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

== Historical investigation ==
=== On the continuity between Je and Chin === In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
=== Comparison of the tombs === During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Sakai|Sakai City]], [[Habikino|Habikino City]], [[Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Tumulus Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Tumulus Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[Emperor Ingyo]]).
Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} excavated from the {{Ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama burial mound]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama burial mound|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

{{Infobox royalty | title = [[King of Wa]] | predecessor = [[Chin of Wa]] | successor = [[Ko of Wa]] | issue = [[Ko of Wa]], [[Bu of Wa]] | posthumous name = [[Emperor Ingyo]]? }} {{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}} {{Five kings of Wa}}
'''Sai of Wa''' ('''済''') was a king of [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}
Father of [[Ko of Wa|Ko]] and [[Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].
== Records ==
=== Book of Liang ===
; [[Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]] : In the [[Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。
=== History of the Southern Dynasties === In the [[History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles. {{Five kings of Wa Sources}}
== Historical investigation ==
=== On the continuity between Je and Chin === In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
=== Comparison of the tombs === During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Sakai|Sakai City]], [[Habikino|Habikino City]], [[Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Tumulus Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Tumulus Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[Emperor Ingyo]]).
Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} excavated from the {{Ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama burial mound]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama burial mound|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

Father of [[Ko of Wa|Ko]] and [[Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].
== Records ==
=== Book of Liang ===
; [[Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]] : In the [[Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。
=== History of the Southern Dynasties === In the [[History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles. {{Five kings of Wa Sources}}
== Historical investigation ==
=== On the continuity between Je and Chin === In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
=== Comparison of the tombs === During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Sakai|Sakai City]], [[Habikino|Habikino City]], [[Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Tumulus Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Tumulus Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[Emperor Ingyo]]).
Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} excavated from the {{Ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama burial mound]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama burial mound|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

revision.comment: Text replacement - "burial mound" to "Kofun"
revision.text:
{{Infobox royalty | title = [[King of Wa]] | predecessor = [[Chin of Wa]] | successor = [[Ko of Wa]] | issue = [[Ko of Wa]], [[Bu of Wa]] | posthumous name = [[Emperor Ingyo]]? }} {{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}} {{Five kings of Wa}}
'''Sai of Wa''' ('''済''') was a king of [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}
Father of [[Ko of Wa|Ko]] and [[Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].
== Records ==
=== Book of Liang ===
; [[Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]] : In the [[Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。
=== History of the Southern Dynasties === In the [[History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles. {{Five kings of Wa Sources}}
== Historical investigation ==
=== On the continuity between Je and Chin === In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
=== Comparison of the tombs === During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Sakai|Sakai City]], [[Habikino|Habikino City]], [[Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Tumulus Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Tumulus Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[Emperor Ingyo]]).
Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} excavated from the {{Ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Bibliography ==
* 事典類 ** {{Cite book |last= |title=日本人名大辞典 |publisher=[[講談社]] |year= |isbn= |editor-last= |chapter=倭王済 |ref={{Harvid|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}}} ** {{Cite book |last=坂元義種 |title=[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] |publisher=[[吉川弘文館]] |year= |isbn= |editor-last= |chapter=倭の五王 |ref={{Harvid|倭の五王（国史）}}}} ** {{Cite book |last= |title=[[日本大百科全書]]（ニッポニカ） |publisher=[[小学館]] |year= |isbn= |editor-last= |chapter= }} *** 坂元義種 「倭の五王」、「済」。 ** {{Cite book |last=坂元義種 |title=日本古代史大辞典 |publisher=[[大和書房]] |year=2006 |isbn=978-4479840657 |editor-last= |chapter=倭の五王 |ref={{Harvid|倭の五王（古代史）|2006}}}} ** {{Cite book |last=関和彦 |title=朝日日本歴史人物事典 |publisher=[[朝日新聞出版]] |year= |isbn= |editor-last= |chapter= 倭王済|chapter-url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%80%AD%E7%8E%8B%E6%B8%88-1121323|ref={{Harvid|倭王済（朝日日本歴史人物事典）}} |author-link=関和彦}} - リンクは朝日新聞社「コトバンク」。 * その他文献 ** {{Cite book |last=森公章 |title=倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002） |publisher=[[山川出版社]] |year=2010 |isbn=978-4634548022 |editor-last= |chapter= |ref={{Harvid|森公章|2010}} |author-link=森公章}} ** {{Cite book |last=河内春人 |title=倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-（[[中公新書]]2470） |publisher=[[中央公論新社]] |year=2018 |isbn=978-4121024701 |editor-last= |chapter= |ref={{Harvid|河内春人|2018}}}} ** {{Cite news |last=[[石井正敏]] |date=June 2005 |title=5世紀の日韓関係 - 倭の五王と高句麗・百済 - |work=日韓歴史共同研究報告書（第1期） |publisher=[[日韓歴史共同研究]] |url=http://www.jkcf.or.jp/history_arch/first/1/1-03-ishii_j.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151018092747/http://www.jkcf.or.jp/history_arch/first/1/1-03-ishii_j.pdf |archive-date=2015-10-18 |ref={{Harvid|石井|2005}}}}

Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} excavated from the {{Ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

revision.text:
{{Infobox royalty | title = [[King of Wa]] | predecessor = [[Chin of Wa]] | successor = [[Ko of Wa]] | issue = [[Ko of Wa]], [[Bu of Wa]] | posthumous name = [[Emperor Ingyo]]? }} {{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}} {{Five kings of Wa}}
'''Sai of Wa''' ('''済''') was a king of [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}
Father of [[Ko of Wa|Ko]] and [[Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].
== Records ==
=== Book of Liang ===
; [[Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]] : In the [[Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。
=== History of the Southern Dynasties === In the [[History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles. {{Five kings of Wa Sources}}
== Historical investigation ==
=== On the continuity between Je and Chin === In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
=== Comparison of the tombs === During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Sakai|Sakai City]], [[Habikino|Habikino City]], [[Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[Emperor Ingyo]]).
Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} excavated from the {{Ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
== References ==
{{Reflist}}

'''Sai of Wa''' ('''済''') was a king of [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}
Father of [[Ko of Wa|Ko]] and [[Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].
== Records ==
=== Book of Liang ===
; [[Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]] : In the [[Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。
=== History of the Southern Dynasties === In the [[History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles. {{Five kings of Wa Sources}}
== Historical investigation ==
=== On the continuity between Je and Chin === In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
=== Comparison of the tombs === During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Sakai|Sakai City]], [[Habikino|Habikino City]], [[Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[Emperor Ingyo]]).
Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} excavated from the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Bibliography ==
* 事典類 ** {{Cite book |last= |title=日本人名大辞典 |publisher=[[講談社]] |year= |isbn= |editor-last= |chapter=倭王済 |ref={{Harvid|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}}} ** {{Cite book |last=坂元義種 |title=[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] |publisher=[[吉川弘文館]] |year= |isbn= |editor-last= |chapter=倭の五王 |ref={{Harvid|倭の五王（国史）}}}} ** {{Cite book |last= |title=[[日本大百科全書]]（ニッポニカ） |publisher=[[小学館]] |year= |isbn= |editor-last= |chapter= }} *** 坂元義種 「倭の五王」、「済」。 ** {{Cite book |last=坂元義種 |title=日本古代史大辞典 |publisher=[[大和書房]] |year=2006 |isbn=978-4479840657 |editor-last= |chapter=倭の五王 |ref={{Harvid|倭の五王（古代史）|2006}}}} ** {{Cite book |last=関和彦 |title=朝日日本歴史人物事典 |publisher=[[朝日新聞出版]] |year= |isbn= |editor-last= |chapter= 倭王済|chapter-url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%80%AD%E7%8E%8B%E6%B8%88-1121323|ref={{Harvid|倭王済（朝日日本歴史人物事典）}} |author-link=関和彦}} - リンクは朝日新聞社「コトバンク」。 * その他文献 ** {{Cite book |last=森公章 |title=倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002） |publisher=[[山川出版社]] |year=2010 |isbn=978-4634548022 |editor-last= |chapter= |ref={{Harvid|森公章|2010}} |author-link=森公章}} ** {{Cite book |last=河内春人 |title=倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-（[[中公新書]]2470） |publisher=[[中央公論新社]] |year=2018 |isbn=978-4121024701 |editor-last= |chapter= |ref={{Harvid|河内春人|2018}}}} ** {{Cite news |last=[[石井正敏]] |date=June 2005 |title=5世紀の日韓関係 - 倭の五王と高句麗・百済 - |work=日韓歴史共同研究報告書（第1期） |publisher=[[日韓歴史共同研究]] |url=http://www.jkcf.or.jp/history_arch/first/1/1-03-ishii_j.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151018092747/http://www.jkcf.or.jp/history_arch/first/1/1-03-ishii_j.pdf |archive-date=2015-10-18 |ref={{Harvid|石井|2005}}}}

{{Infobox royalty | title = [[King of Wa]] | predecessor = [[Chin of Wa]] | successor = [[Ko of Wa]] | issue = [[Ko of Wa]], [[Bu of Wa]] | posthumous name = [[Emperor Ingyo]]? }} {{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}} {{Five kings of Wa}}
'''Sai of Wa''' ('''済''') was a king of [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}
Father of [[Ko of Wa|Ko]] and [[Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].
== Records ==
=== Book of Liang ===
; [[Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]] : In the [[Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。

== Historical investigation ==

Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
=== Comparison of the tombs === During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Sakai|Sakai City]], [[Habikino|Habikino City]], [[Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[Emperor Ingyo]]).
Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} excavated from the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

{{Infobox royalty | title = [[King of Wa]] | predecessor = [[Chin of Wa]] | successor = [[Ko of Wa]] | issue = [[Ko of Wa]], [[Bu of Wa]] | posthumous name = [[Emperor Ingyo]]? }} {{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}} {{Five kings of Wa}}
'''Sai of Wa''' ('''済''') was a king of [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}
Father of [[Ko of Wa|Ko]] and [[Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].
== Records ==
=== Book of Liang ===
; [[Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]] : In the [[Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。
=== History of the Southern Dynasties === In the [[History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles. {{Five kings of Wa Sources}}
== Historical investigation ==
=== On the continuity between Je and Chin === In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
=== Comparison of the tombs === During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Sakai|Sakai City]], [[Habikino|Habikino City]], [[Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[Emperor Ingyo]]).
Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} excavated from the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

{{Infobox royalty | title = [[King of Wa]] | predecessor = [[Chin of Wa]] | successor = [[Ko of Wa]] | issue = [[Ko of Wa]], [[Bu of Wa]] | posthumous name = [[Emperor Ingyo]]? }} {{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}} {{Five kings of Wa}}
'''Sai of Wa''' ('''済''') was a king of [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}
Father of [[Ko of Wa|Ko]] and [[Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].
== Records ==
=== Book of Liang ===
; [[Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]] : In the [[Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。
=== History of the Southern Dynasties === In the [[History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles. {{Five kings of Wa Sources}}
== Historical investigation ==
=== On the continuity between Je and Chin === In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
=== Comparison of the tombs === During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Sakai|Sakai City]], [[Habikino|Habikino City]], [[Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[Emperor Ingyo]]).
Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} excavated from the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sai of Wa]][[ja:済]][[zh:济 (倭王)]]
{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Wakoku|qid=Q10888424}}|children={{ill|Bu|qid=Q28692301}}; {{ill|Kō|qid=Q28692300}}|organization={{ill|Five kings of Wa|qid=Q1152880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q28692299]]
Wikidata name: Sei
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox royalty
| title        = [[:en:King of Wa|King of Wa]]
| predecessor  = [[:en:Chin of Wa|Chin of Wa]]
| successor    = [[Kō of Wa|Ko of Wa]]
| issue        = [[Kō of Wa|Ko of Wa]], [[:en:Bu of Wa|Bu of Wa]]
| posthumous name = [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]?
}}
{{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}}
{{Five kings of Wa}}

'''Sai of Wa'''  ('''済''') was a king of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[:en:5th century|5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}

Father of [[Kō of Wa|Ko]] and [[:en:Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].

== Records ==

=== Book of Liang ===

; [[:en:Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]]
: In the  [[:en:Book of Liang|Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。

=== History of the Southern Dynasties ===
In the [[:en:History of the Southern Dynasties|History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles.
{{Five kings of Wa Sources}}

== Historical investigation ==

=== On the continuity between Je and Chin ===
In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wazui|qq=|1=Wa Zui|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55523253}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

=== Comparison of the tombs ===
During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], [[:en:Fujiidera|Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[:en:Ichinoyama Kofun|Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]).

Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Sword|qq=|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} excavated from the {{ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Kofun Group|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Inaridai No. 1 Kofun|WD=Q11596847}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eta Funayama Sword|qq=|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sai of Wa]][[ja:済]][[zh:济 (倭王)]]
{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424}}|children={{ill|Bu|WD=Q28692301}}; {{ill|Kō|WD=Q28692300}}|organization={{ill|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q28692299]]
Wikidata name: Sei
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox royalty
| title        = [[:en:King of Wa|King of Wa]]
| predecessor  = [[:en:Chin of Wa|Chin of Wa]]
| successor    = [[Kō of Wa|Ko of Wa]]
| issue        = [[Kō of Wa|Ko of Wa]], [[:en:Bu of Wa|Bu of Wa]]
| posthumous name = [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]?
}}
{{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}}
{{Five kings of Wa}}

'''Sai of Wa'''  ('''済''') was a king of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[:en:5th century|5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}

Father of [[Kō of Wa|Ko]] and [[:en:Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].

== Records ==

=== Book of Liang ===

; [[:en:Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]]
: In the  [[:en:Book of Liang|Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。

=== History of the Southern Dynasties ===
In the [[:en:History of the Southern Dynasties|History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles.
{{Five kings of Wa Sources}}

== Historical investigation ==

=== On the continuity between Je and Chin ===
In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wazui|qq=|1=Wa Zui|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55523253}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

=== Comparison of the tombs ===
During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], [[:en:Fujiidera|Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[:en:Ichinoyama Kofun|Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]).

Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Sword|qq=|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} excavated from the {{ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Kofun Group|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Inaridai No. 1 Kofun|WD=Q11596847}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eta Funayama Sword|qq=|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], [[:en:Fujiidera|Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[:en:Ichinoyama Kofun|Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]).

Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Sword|qq=|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} excavated from the {{ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Kofun Group|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Inaridai No. 1 Kofun|WD=Q11596847}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eta Funayama Sword|qq=|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

{{Infobox royalty
| title        = [[:en:King of Wa|King of Wa]]
| predecessor  = [[:en:Chin of Wa|Chin of Wa]]
| successor    = [[Kō of Wa|Ko of Wa]]
| issue        = [[Kō of Wa|Ko of Wa]], [[:en:Bu of Wa|Bu of Wa]]
| posthumous name = [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]?

{{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}}
{{Five kings of Wa}}

'''Sai of Wa'''  ('''済''') was a king of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[:en:5th century|5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}

Father of [[Kō of Wa|Ko]] and [[:en:Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].

== Records ==

=== Book of Liang ===

; [[:en:Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]]
: In the  [[:en:Book of Liang|Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。

=== History of the Southern Dynasties ===
In the [[:en:History of the Southern Dynasties|History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles.
{{Five kings of Wa Sources}}

== Historical investigation ==

=== On the continuity between Je and Chin ===
In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wazui|1=Wa Zui|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55523253}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

=== Comparison of the tombs ===
During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], [[:en:Fujiidera|Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[:en:Ichinoyama Kofun|Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]).

Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Sword|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} excavated from the {{ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Kofun Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Inaridai No. 1 Kofun|WD=Q11596847}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eta Funayama Sword|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sai of Wa]][[ja:済]][[zh:济 (倭王)]]
{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424}}|children={{ill|Bu|WD=Q28692301}}; {{ill|Kō|WD=Q28692300}}|organization={{ill|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q28692299]]
Wikidata name: Sei
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox royalty
| title        = [[:en:King of Wa|King of Wa]]
| predecessor  = [[:en:Chin of Wa|Chin of Wa]]
| successor    = [[Kō of Wa|Ko of Wa]]
| issue        = [[Kō of Wa|Ko of Wa]], [[:en:Bu of Wa|Bu of Wa]]
| posthumous name = [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]?
}}
{{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}}
{{Five kings of Wa}}

'''Sai of Wa'''  ('''済''') was a king of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[:en:5th century|5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}

Father of [[Kō of Wa|Ko]] and [[:en:Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].

== Records ==

=== Book of Liang ===

; [[:en:Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]]
: In the  [[:en:Book of Liang|Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。

=== History of the Southern Dynasties ===
In the [[:en:History of the Southern Dynasties|History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles.
{{Five kings of Wa Sources}}

== Historical investigation ==

=== On the continuity between Je and Chin ===
In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wazui|1=Wa Zui|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55523253}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

=== Comparison of the tombs ===
During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], [[:en:Fujiidera|Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[:en:Ichinoyama Kofun|Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]).

Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Sword|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} excavated from the {{ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Kofun Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Inaridai No. 1 Kofun|WD=Q11596847}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eta Funayama Sword|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424}}|children={{ill|Bu|WD=Q28692301}}; {{ill|Kō|WD=Q28692300}}|organization={{ill|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q28692299]]

{{Infobox royalty
| title        = [[:en:King of Wa|King of Wa]]
| predecessor  = [[:en:Chin of Wa|Chin of Wa]]
| successor    = [[Kō of Wa|Ko of Wa]]
| issue        = [[Kō of Wa|Ko of Wa]], [[:en:Bu of Wa|Bu of Wa]]
| posthumous name = [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]?
}}
{{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}}
{{Five kings of Wa}}

'''Sai of Wa'''  ('''済''') was a king of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[:en:5th century|5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}

Father of [[Kō of Wa|Ko]] and [[:en:Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].

== Records ==

=== Book of Liang ===

; [[:en:Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]]
: In the  [[:en:Book of Liang|Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。

=== History of the Southern Dynasties ===
In the [[:en:History of the Southern Dynasties|History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles.
{{Five kings of Wa Sources}}

== Historical investigation ==

=== On the continuity between Je and Chin ===
In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wazui|1=Wa Zui|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55523253}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

=== Comparison of the tombs ===
During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], [[:en:Fujiidera|Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[:en:Ichinoyama Kofun|Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]).

Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Sword|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} excavated from the {{ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Kofun Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Inaridai No. 1 Kofun|WD=Q11596847}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eta Funayama Sword|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wazui|1=Wa Zui|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55523253}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

=== Comparison of the tombs ===
During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], [[:en:Fujiidera|Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[:en:Ichinoyama Kofun|Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]).

Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Sword|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} excavated from the {{ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Kofun Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Inaridai No. 1 Kofun|WD=Q11596847}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eta Funayama Sword|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

{{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}}
{{Five kings of Wa}}

'''Sai of Wa'''  ('''済''') was a king of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[:en:5th century|5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}

Father of [[Kō of Wa|Ko]] and [[:en:Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].

== Records ==

=== Book of Liang ===

; [[:en:Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]]
: In the  [[:en:Book of Liang|Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。

=== History of the Southern Dynasties ===
In the [[:en:History of the Southern Dynasties|History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles.
{{Five kings of Wa Sources}}

{{Infobox royalty
| title        = [[:en:King of Wa|King of Wa]]
| predecessor  = [[:en:Chin of Wa|Chin of Wa]]
| successor    = [[Kō of Wa|Ko of Wa]]
| issue        = [[Kō of Wa|Ko of Wa]], [[:en:Bu of Wa|Bu of Wa]]
| posthumous name = [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]?
}}
{{Short description|One of the five kings of Wa}}
{{Five kings of Wa}}

'''Sai of Wa'''  ('''済''') was a king of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the middle of the [[:en:5th century|5th century]] ([[Kofun period|Kofun era]]).{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}} He was also known as '''Wa Osai'''.{{Sfn|倭王済（日本人名大辞典）}}

Father of [[Kō of Wa|Ko]] and [[:en:Bu of Wa|Wu]], he was one of the ''[[Five kings of Wa|Wa]]''. He is considered to be the 19th [[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyo]].

== Records ==

=== Book of Liang ===

; [[:en:Book of Liang|The Biography of Liang]]
: In the  [[:en:Book of Liang|Book of Liang]], the article on "Yamato" (梁書倭伝) states that his son Je stood up after the death of "Ya", and his son Xing stood up after his death.<ref name="東洋文庫 梁書">{{Cite book |last=井上秀雄 |title=東アジア民族史 1-正史東夷伝 |date=1974-12-01 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |isbn=4582802648 |series=[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264 |pages=315–319 |author-link=井上秀雄}}</ref>。

=== History of the Southern Dynasties ===
In the [[:en:History of the Southern Dynasties|History of the Southern Dynasties]], the article on the Japanese Kingdom (Nan shi wagu den) describes the contents of the Song Shu Chronicles.
{{Five kings of Wa Sources}}

== Historical investigation ==

=== On the continuity between Je and Chin ===
In the article in the Sung Shu, Je takes the surname "Wa" (倭) as did the previous Japanese king Jin, but does not clarify his relationship to Jin. Since they do not name the continuation, there is a theory that Je and Chin were not close blood relatives, and since the [[Nihon Shoki]] shows a struggle in the succession to the throne after [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], the possibility of the existence of such a struggle over the throne is pointed out {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}} In addition, since the {{ill|Wazui|ja|倭隋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wazui|1=Wa Zui|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55523253}} can be seen as a particularly powerful royal family in the Chin period, there is a theory that there were two royal forces at that time ([[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]), and that Je may have been a lineage of this Wazui {{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

=== Comparison of the tombs ===
During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], [[:en:Fujiidera|Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[:en:Ichinoyama Kofun|Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]).

Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Sword|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} excavated from the {{ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Kofun Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Inaridai No. 1 Kofun|WD=Q11596847}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eta Funayama Sword|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

During the period of activity of the [[Five kings of Wa]], the tombs of the great kings were constructed in the [[Mozu Tombs]] and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], [[:en:Fujiidera|Fujiidera]]), and Je's tomb is assumed to be one of them.<ref>[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/story/ "Wa" no Monogatari] (Headquarters Committee for the Promotion of the Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group World Cultural Heritage Registration, "Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Group")</ref> These tombs are now designated as mausoleums by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Miyouchi Agency]], so there is a lack of archaeological data to date them, but one theory compares them to the [[:en:Ichinoyama Kofun|Ichinoyama Kofun]] (the current imperial tomb of [[:en:Emperor Ingyo|Emperor Ingyo]]).

Another archaeological source is the {{ill|Inaridai Sword|lt="Gift of Wang"|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Sword|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} excavated from the {{ill|Inaridai Kofun Group|ja|稲荷台1号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai Kofun Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Inaridai No. 1 Kofun|WD=Q11596847}} (Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture). (or Chin), since he is self-explanatory only by writing "Wang".{{Sfn|Mori Kosho|2010|pp=51-55}}{{Sfn|Kouchi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}} It should be noted, however, that the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|iron sword from Inariyama Kofun]] inscription and the {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|lt=iron sword from Etafuneyama Kofun|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eta Funayama Sword|1=Iron swords and iron sword inscriptions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649534}} inscription are distinct from the "Great King" of the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
